speaker: interviewer
text: February, 1995. Survivor, Isidore Hollander. Interviewer, Frances Aronovitz. City, Miami. Country, United States of America. Language, English. Take one. Please state your name and spell your name for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Isidore Hollander. I-S-A-D-O-R-E H-O-L-L-A-N-D-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hollander. And my Yiddish nomer was Yitzhak Doff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Hollander as it was spelled when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When I was  born, it was spelled in French. Actually, my name-- my parents' name was the spelling H-O-L-E-N-D-E-R, I was born in Paris. And my mother received me after birth with my birth certificate, H-O-L-L-A-N-E-D-E-R. And coming to the United States, we changed the name as it is now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the year of your birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, you are how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to be 75 July 26.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents came to Paris 1919. I was born 1920. And then, my father became ill. He was told that he is not going to survive. Those years was very hardship in France, in Paris, to be buried in a Jewish cemetery. So as my father was raised as an orthodox, he wanted to go back home to Poland. A matter of fact, when he left and I was age three-- 1923-- he only lived two weeks, and he passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that when you moved to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why we stayed in Poland. My mother never went back to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what life was like for you in Poland when you were young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were coming back to Poland. Four years-- I was with my mother up to age seven. And my mother went through with us, with me and a sister of mine-- an older sister, which she's perished-- a lot of not good four years. And finally, she found somebody to get married after being four years a widow. And it happen the stepfather wasn't too much agreeable with my mother to keep us at home, me and my sister. So my mother gave me away to an orphanage, to a Jewish orphanage, in hometown of Bendin. Which, thanks to those good years that I was in the orphanage, I was raised as a mensch. I was educated in Yiddish and the Polish language. In the 1930s, we possessed in the orphanage already a Chumash-- translated in Polish like, we have in here in English. And we used to follow the codex of Janusz Korczak, the one that went with his children in Warsaw with the orphanages to Treblinka. And we were raised very well-disciplined. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I didn't have a father, but I don't regret that I was in the orphanage. Because who knows would have-- what come out out of me when I would have been with my stepfather. Well, I finished public school in the orphanage. I finished three years evening school. That's part of high school. And I learned a trade-- I learned to be a tailor, ladies' garments. And I worked till the end of-- till the beginning of the War, till 1939. And I left the orphanage. I already start to make a little living. So Hitler came in and interrupted young life, age 19. I was told a story before, a little story, that 1936, as I was born in France, and I had aunts and friends with cousins-- which I was twice visiting them after the war, my mother's sisters-- and I was trying to get away from Poland, because we knew already that we have a enemy. Hitler-- we start to read about, get educated about. So I figured, maybe I'll go away from Poland. I'll go to France. And I smuggled myself. And I was caught in a German town, Breslau. And I was taken to the police. And an officer in the brown shorts slapped me in my face. And he told me that we have enough the Jews-- swines in here. We don't need another one. You go back. He slapped me. And he go back and he said, tell your friends and the other swines that we will follow. Well, I couldn't-- I couldn't go back the same path I went. But I had to go back to Czechoslovakia. When I came back, I used to belong to a Zionist organization, [NON-ENGLISH]. When I start to tell them what I was told in Germany, when I was slapped by going back, they thought that I'm bringing propaganda and I'm going to panic the Jewish people. And nobody believed that somebody like me-- 16 years of age-- could come up with such a lie-- that is what I was told. But unfortunate, it happened that they did follow. And it was too late to believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go back to the orphanage for just a minute-- did your mother visit? Did you see your mother while you were in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We went home every Saturday afternoon, all the children go home to their parents. We had to come back in the evening, up to age 14. 15, we slept in the orphanage-- but as of limited space. When we become 14, they located us to Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's say, was a-- a woman, a widow-- she had an extra room, they paid for it from the Jewish community that she gave us-- like, me and another friend, not far from the orphanage, which we located. But food and all the other things-- clothing-- we had in the orphanage. And at nighttime, we went to sleep over there. Was your sister also placed in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my sister was already four years older than I am. But she had a bad life. She had to get married at 17, because of the stepfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the orphanage, did you live a Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very Jewish orthodox life. A matter of fact, two-- two and a half-- two and a half years ago, I went back to Poland to my hometown, with my son. He wanted to know our bitterness-- from where we are coming. And I went to that orphanage. It is a hospital now. But I have it on videotape. Yes. I went there. I spoke to the doctors over there. And I would like that we Jewish people should come and invest money. They need it badly. [SIGHS] They needed us badly. But they didn't need us when the time was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be with them, they didn't need us. Now, they want us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends among the Gentile community when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had friends. But the same friend who was my good friend-- a Gentile-- and Sunday, when they go to church, you see him standing in front of the church and handing out leaflets-- anti-Semitic leaflets. When I came by, he says, I don't mean you. He didn't mean me. Because he thought he was my friend. But he wasn't well-educated to say that. But I have to tell you, my blood is soaked-- my body-- with anti-Semitism all my life. Since I began to understand who I am and who was behind me, it wasn't peaches and cream. We couldn't go, in Poland, into a Gentile community and being beat up by the youth-- by the Polish youth calling Jew, dirty Jew, throwing stones. In school, we had a separate school. It was a public school. But majority was Jewish schools. And Gentile schools were separated. Because in Poland, we learned religion in school. And the last two years before the war broke out, they were trying to mix. So the Jewish-born kids went to the Gentile school. Every day, somebody was beat up. When they came to our school, we didn't know how to do that. We're Jewish kids. We didn't carry on hatred. But we felt very uncomfortable between the Polish people, very uncomfortable. I was many times attacked by the orphanage, coming from someplace. And they were standing around-- youth, anti-Semites, and attacking us, chasing us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do when they attacked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they could have beat us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a couple times. But I got out of danger. You know, I knew how to handle myself. I couldn't fight back. But I got out of it. I tried to do the best to get out of it. I learned to be a survivor from a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish community-- philanthropists. We had quite a few philanthropists in our city. I have a book from our city with the pictures of those people, wealthy people. They kept the Jewish community in place. They came every Saturday. They walked to the orphanage. And they helped serve us with our Shabbos dinner. It's-- it's-- it's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's unforgettable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of dreams did you have for your future at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I dreamt to become somebody, to learn a trade and, hopefully, you know, grow up, live in Poland-- as we lived-- situated, get married, family. But I was too young to think of those things. I wasn't enough established. Because when you are away from a parent, and how much you are guided wherever you are, but you couldn't get-- you blocked-- relished guiding from a mother. It was very hard for me. Which my mother had two children with her second marriage-- from her second marriage. And we were very close, but still, I missed a home. You know-- wherever you are, you miss a mother. But I have seen her every week, you know. We were close, as much as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their home like?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish home. The stepfather, the second husband, wasn't home that much. He was, like, a salesman for something and used to be-- most of the time-- out, selling whatever. I don't know what he did too much. She didn't have such a good life. She had a good life and a short life by her first husband, my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very jealous of her being-- getting married. But who am I? Who was I, seven years of age, to tell a young wife and a young woman how to act their life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up. I began to understand her needs, her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the first time that you had an inkling of what was to come, regarding the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was involved in the orphanage. And I was always active and I was always aware of myself. And by reading a newspaper, a Yiddish paper, an English paper, a Polish paper, we start to sense what the meaning [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of Hitler. What is-- it's coming. What it might come. And gradually, I couldn't-- we couldn't-- think how to react till the time came. Nobody wanted to believe that it will happen so fast. We thought most that it will come, Hitler will start a war or whatever. But we didn't know that it will come to Poland so fast, which we lived close to the German border-- 14 kilometers, which is like 10 miles, nine miles. And right away, to begin to execute Jewish rich people, and orders, you know were to liquidate this, to liquidate that, and then deliver this and deliver other things, and start to catch people to hard labor, to work. Not to send away to camps yet. But if you didn't fill our their orders, if you didn't obey, and if you didn't fulfill the curfew time, you risk you life to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did this start?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, September 1, Hitler invaded Poland. And September 3, they were in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had restrictions on when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately, they gave restrictions. They took over the Jewish community. The only thing what they did-- they were so clever in politics to do that they formed a Jewish police, you know. That we Jewish people, among us, we created panic. You know, a Jewish police-- we'd say that, well, the German didn't arrest me. The Jewish police arrested you. You know, they gave orders, which you couldn't be helped. Everybody would try to make a way not to be caught and not to get involved to get hurt. And everybody-- anybody-- wants to live. If anything goes into a panic in life, you'll see, people want to live. And if you ever heard about what happened in the gas chambers, when they start to let the gas through, the Zyklon B, people were running. People were climbing one on top of the other. People scratched their bodies one on the other. And everybody wanted to live, to see how to get out of there. And unfortunate-- happened to us also true. We couldn't fight through that. But we had uprisings in our city, a couple uprisings in the ghettos. Mordechai Anielewicz came to our city twice from Warsaw, organizing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he came in '42. I wasn't home already. See, I was already out. Because in '39, near the end, I was caught to a labor-- to labor in a coal mine in Jaworzno, not far from Bedzin.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were taken to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taken and sent to a coal mine not far. And the city, Jaworzno, was J-A-W-O-R-Z-N-O. That's in Polish spelling, Jaworzno. And I was young, 19. And looks like, to have a little think, you have to have a little logo, like they say in the English language, 'guts.' And I formed myself with a few more fellows, and we escaped. But I couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Escaped from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the coal mine. It was not a labor camp-- it wasn't a concentration camp. They kept us in barracks, you know, overnight. And in the morning, under guard, we went to work in the coal mines.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we escaped. But I couldn't go home anymore. Because they already knew my address. When they caught us, they took my name and my home. So I came home for one in the middle of the night. And I said to my mother, get me one of the sisters-- my stepsisters with my second father. She didn't want it.

speaker: interviewee
text: She blessed me. She says, go. And God should be with you. And save yourself as much as you can.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I escaped. And I escaped to the Russian side. At that time, Russia already had taken over half Poland. So I escaped to a town, Pshemish, crossing the border. When I crossed the San River, the Germans were shooting behind and the Russians were shooting in the front. Luck was the air. And the next day, in the morning, I wind up in a prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the KGB already began investigating us. Which, that time, they still called themselves the NKVD, before they changed their name, KGB. And while I was in the prison, I saw a friend of mine already, from Bendin, sitting in there in a cell. And he yelled out to me, don't tell that you were a communist! Well, I was never a communist. But the reason a lot of people told the Russians that they were communist, they were the ones were sent to Siberia immediately. Because Russia had the politics not to trust anybody from outside Russia. They say, if you were a communist, fight where you are. Don't come to us. We don't need you. They didn't trust. Well, they let us out. And a few times, I was sent. I went to Lvov, another city from Pshemish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1940 already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was after you had escaped from the coal mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I escaped from the coal mine, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And crossed to the Russian side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian side. I was on the Russian side. I went to [NON-ENGLISH]. And then, they organized us and they sent us again to coal mines in Donbas. And in there, they let me go down to a shift. And they gave me go punch a hole in the wall and the coal. And I remember when I start to slide in in that foxhole, I lost my galoshes. And I walked out. I says, kill me. I'm not going back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they send me up, and I walked up, up on top on the grounds. And didn't last long. And I ran away again. I run. But I couldn't help it. I was arrested by the Russians. And I was sent to Siberia to a concentration camp. Why were you arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got ten years for being a counter-revolutionary. Because my father had a little business on a market. So I was a capitalist. With them, you don't play. They did what they had to do, what they want to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't last long. I was in Siberia into the 19-- when the war started, 1941, with Russia-- German-Russia. In 1942, at the end, I escaped. From Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle, in '38, I escaped from Siberia. And it's very hard to describe how I did it. I went through a lot of hardship. And I start to run back into the Polish territory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any help when you escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had help, a truck driver. Which I was a lucky man, to begin with, that I was very liked by people. I was in a hospital in concentration camp in Siberia. I work for the doctors. I used to prepare all the dead people for autopsies and buried them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a pass that I could go out of the camp, 20 kilometers-- you know, an area of 20 kilometers square. And I got to know one of the drivers who delivered to the camps. And looks like he was an understanding person. He helped me to escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Russia, if you get out from one state to another, they-- it's very hard to catch up with the person. But you cannot ride on the train without documents. You cannot cross the Volga without a document. And in the year, I had to go about 4,000 miles over to come back where I wanted. And here the war goes on between Russia and Germany. And I smuggled myself and I came back into Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you traveling alone at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: All alone. I didn't want anybody to know who I am. I spoke Russian very well. Found myself to survive. I cannot describe what I ate, how I live through, and circumstances. Because later there were wars what I did now. When I came into the underground, I came into a town, to Rovno, a Jewish-dominated town in Poland. And I got myself into the ghetto, which the Jewish people didn't like it. You know, we Jewish people, how much we want for each other. But if a stranger came in, we didn't trust each other. It's normal things. And overheard, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] overheard in the ghetto that the ghetto will be liquidated. So I got myself together again with a few fellows and we made a escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ghetto were you in at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rovno, town of Rovno, in the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Ukraine, after you escaped from Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Siberia. I came back into-- from a concentration camp into the ghetto. I escaped from the ghetto. And then, I didn't have any other choice. To be caught by the Germans and get killed-- those people in those areas were Sobibor, or the other camps, which they gassed you immediately. They-- hardly, people from those places lived through.
